8 min read
Just now

--

A practical look at Deep Data Security, Data Redaction, and post-quantum TLS
The SQL Firewall article covered one layer of defense: guaranteeing that only approved SQL runs against the database. Three problems sit outside that guarantee. Approved SQL needs an identity behind it: a specific, verifiable person, rather than a shared database session. A query returning the right row to the wrong person, because every user shares the same connection, is a failure Firewall was never built to catch. And algorithms that hold up against classical computers today can still leave traffic readable to a quantum computer twenty years from now.
Oracle AI Database 26ai addresses each of these with a distinct capability: Deep Data Security for identity-aware authorization, Data Redaction for value-level masking, and post-quantum cryptography for the wire and the disk. All three build on top of SQL Firewall, and each protects against a different failure: a system can pass every SQL Firewall check, mask nothing, and still leak a value nobody was authorized to see.
Moving authorization into the database with Deep Data Security
A conventional application connects to Oracle through one privileged database account shared by every end user, and pushes authorization decisions into application code. A developer writes the filter that limits an employee to their own HR record, someone reviews it, it ships. The model works because the queries are predictable and the code path is auditable.
Agentic AI removes that predictability. When an agent builds SQL at runtime to answer a user’s question, there is no fixed query for anyone to review beforehand, and the agent still connects through the same privileged account the application always used. Oracle made this explicit in the announcement accompanying Deep Data Security’s general availability in 26ai: agents inherit the account’s full access, while leaving behind the discipline of a predefined query and the authorization logic that used to live in application code.
Deep Data Security (Deep Sec) moves that authorization into the database itself. It extends ideas Oracle DBAs already know from Virtual Private Database and Real Application Security, replacing PL/SQL-heavy policy functions with a declarative SQL object: the data grant.
A data grant is created with CREATE DATA GRANT. It reads close to a regular GRANT statement, except that it can restrict access down to individual columns, and the restriction is expressed as a WHERE predicate evaluated against the caller's identity at runtime rather than against a static role.
CREATE OR REPLACE DATA GRANT hr.manager_direct_reports
AS SELECT (ALL COLUMNS EXCEPT ssn), UPDATE (salary)
ON hr.employees
WHERE manager = ORA_END_USER_CONTEXT.username
TO manager_role;This grant lets a manager see every column of their direct reports except SSN, and update only the salary column. If Marvin holds both manager_role and an equivalent grant for his own record, querying hr.employees returns his own row in full and his reports' rows with SSN coming back as NULL instead of an error or a blank field. Deep Sec resolves multiple applicable roles with OR logic, so holding both roles only adds visibility, it never subtracts it.
The identity behind ORA_END_USER_CONTEXT.username doesn't come from a database login. It arrives as an OAuth2 token issued by an external identity provider, typically Microsoft Entra ID or OCI IAM, and gets propagated to the database on the connection through the JDBC, python-oracledb, or ODP.NET driver. Application roles from the identity provider map to Deep Sec data roles inside the database, so there is no need to provision every end user as a database account. python-oracledb 4.0 shipped a dedicated plugin for exactly this, letting a Python application attach an end-user security context to a pooled connection before running a query and clear it afterward.
Checking which data grants already exist on an object is a quick dictionary query:
SELECT owner, object_owner, object_name, grant_name
FROM dba_data_grants
WHERE object_name = 'EMPLOYEES';The owner column identifies the schema that owns the data grant itself, while object_owner identifies the schema of the table or view being protected. The two aren't always the same schema, since one user's data grant can protect an object owned by someone else.
For an architecture where a single connection pool or an MCP server sits between an AI agent and the database, this changes where the authorization logic lives. It stops being a check every new endpoint has to reimplement, and becomes a property of the data itself, enforced the same way no matter what wrote the query.
Data Redaction hides what Deep Sec already approved
Deep Sec decides whether a row or column is visible at all. Data Redaction operates one layer further in: the row is visible, the column is visible, and the value still gets masked before it leaves the database. Take Patricia, a support agent whom Deep Sec authorizes to see every column of a customer’s record, SSN included. Data Redaction still returns her SSN with only the last few digits visible, because a separate redaction policy sits between her authorized access and the value Oracle actually returns.
Data Redaction has existed since Oracle 12c and the core mechanism hasn’t changed: DBMS_REDACT.ADD_POLICY attaches a masking function to a column, and Oracle applies that function to the value at query time without touching the stored data.
BEGIN
DBMS_REDACT.ADD_POLICY(
object_schema => 'hr',
object_name => 'employees',
column_name => 'ssn',
policy_name => 'redact_ssn',
function_type => DBMS_REDACT.PARTIAL,
function_parameters => 'VVVVFVVVFVVVV,VVV-VV-VVVV,*,1,5',
expression => '1=1'
);
END;
/Confirming which columns already carry a policy is just as direct:
SELECT object_owner, object_name, column_name, function_type
FROM redaction_columns
WHERE object_name = 'EMPLOYEES';Pairing that with redaction_policies, which lists the policy name and its enabled state per object, is usually enough to answer "is this column actually being masked right now" without having to log in as a lower-privileged user to test it.
What 23ai and 26ai added is support in places the feature used to reject outright. Older releases raised ORA-28094 the moment a redacted column appeared inside a view, a GROUP BY, a DISTINCT, an ORDER BY, or a set operator, which forced developers to work around redaction instead of relying on it for anything beyond a flat SELECT. That restriction is gone: redacted columns now work inside views and inline views, and DISTINCT, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and UNION all return correctly redacted results instead of erroring out. In practice, that means redaction can now sit underneath the reporting and analytics queries an agent tends to generate on its own, instead of breaking the moment someone adds a GROUP BY.
One caution carries over unchanged from earlier releases: Data Redaction targets read-mostly, call-center-style access. An application that writes back a value it just read as redacted will happily persist the masked characters over the real ones, so it still needs a genuine write path that never goes through the redacted view.
Getting ahead of harvest-now-decrypt-later
The first two layers assume an attacker already inside the query path. This one assumes an attacker who is just listening. Harvest now, decrypt later describes exactly that: record TLS traffic today, encrypted with algorithms like RSA or ECDHE that a sufficiently capable quantum computer could eventually break, and decrypt it once that computer exists. For data with a short shelf life this barely matters. For medical records, financial history, or anything with a multi-decade retention requirement, it means the theft already happened, even if nobody can read the file yet.
Take a health insurance claim stored under a multi-decade retention requirement. If that claim traveled over TLS protected only by ECDHE, and someone captured the traffic in 2026, a working quantum computer in 2035 would hand them a claim that’s still supposed to stay confidential for years afterward.
NIST closed the standards gap on August 13, 2024, finalizing three algorithms after an eight-year evaluation: ML-KEM (FIPS 203) for key exchange, ML-DSA (FIPS 204) for digital signatures, and SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) as a hash-based signature alternative resting on a more conservative security assumption than the lattice-based pair.
Oracle AI Database 26ai was among the first database platforms to ship quantum-safe TLS built on these standards. Two defaults changed with the release: TDE now uses AES-256 for both column and tablespace encryption, where 19c defaulted to AES-192 for columns and AES-128 for tablespaces, and TLS 1.3 gained support for ML-KEM key exchange alongside the existing ECDHE.
Oracle didn’t move straight to pure ML-KEM, and the reasoning is worth keeping in mind when configuring this. ECDHE carries decades of cryptanalysis and no known break. ML-KEM is sound by NIST’s own extensive review, but it is young by cryptographic standards and hasn’t had the same real-world exposure yet. Oracle’s hybrid mode runs both key exchanges in the same handshake and combines the two resulting keys into one session key, so the connection stays protected even if one of the two algorithms turns out to have a flaw nobody has found. Release 26.1 supports both hybrid and pure ML-KEM modes, selectable through the database’s COMPATIBLE setting and the CONFIGURE ENCRYPTION ALGORITHM command.
Turning this on is mostly a matter of sqlnet.ora:
TLS_VERSIONS = (TLSv1.3)
TLS_KEY_EXCHANGE_GROUPS = (hybrid, ml-kem, ec)A single query shows what an existing environment is actually running:
SELECT tablespace_name, encryptionalg
FROM dba_tablespaces
WHERE encrypted = 'YES';Anything still showing AES128 from a database created before the default changed can be moved forward without downtime:
ALTER TABLESPACE users ENCRYPTION ONLINE REKEY USING AES256;None of this requires a quantum computer to exist yet to be worth doing. The traffic being harvested today is the traffic at risk, and the rekey work for a large tablespace is measured in hours or days once it starts, which is reason enough to check the algorithm column now rather than after a compliance deadline forces the question.
What this doesn’t cover yet
Put next to SQL Firewall, these three layers cover a wide surface: what SQL can run, who it runs as, what values come back, and how the wire traffic and the disk stay protected. One case this list doesn’t cover: authorization when an agent acts under its own service identity instead of impersonating an end user. That’s a different problem from the delegated-access case Deep Sec solves here, and it deserves its own article.
For a team modernizing a legacy monolith into microservices that share a schema with the system still in production, sequencing matters. Deep Sec has to come first, because it changes how connections and identity get wired through the application, and every other change depends on that wiring being right. Redaction can wait until then: it doesn’t touch the schema, so nothing about adding it later disturbs work already done. TLS and TDE, meanwhile, barely need sequencing at all. Neither one requires the application to notice anything changed, which means they can move forward on their own timeline, independent of the other two.
References

• Oracle SQL Firewall: Protecting Your Database from Injection in 5 Minutes

• Oracle: Oracle Deep Data Security Is Now Available in Oracle AI Database 26ai (May 2026)

• Oracle Documentation: Oracle Deep Data Security Guide — Create Data Grants

• Sharad Chandran, Oracle Developers (Medium): Using Oracle AI Database Deep Data Security in Python Applications (May 2026)

• ORACLE-BASE: Data Redaction (DBMS_REDACT) Enhancements in Oracle Database 23ai/26ai

• Oracle: Securing Oracle AI Database 26ai for the Quantum Era

• Oracle: Both Is Better — Oracle
